text: CAVRIGLIA, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the comp. of Sienna, 5 m. W of Montevarchi. Pop.
CAW. See KANSAS.
CAWKWELL, a parish of Lincolnshire, 7 m. N of Horncastle. Area 540 acres. Pop. 47.
CAWNPORE. See CAUNPORE.
CAWOOD, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire. 17 m. ENE of Leeds, on the l. bank of the Ouse, which is here crossed by a ferry. Area 2,840 acres. Pop.

CAWSAND BEACON, or CAWSORN, a hill in Devonshire, in the N part of Dartmoor, 1,792 ft. in height above sea-level. It formed one of the stations of the great triangulation on which the Ordnance maps are based.
CAWSTON, a parish of Norfolk, 21 m. E of Reepham, near a branch of the Wensum. Area 4,430 acres. Pop. 1,130.
CAWTHORN, a township in the p. of Middleton, N. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,540 acres. Pop. 20.
CAWTHORNE, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 41 m. NW of Barnesley. Area 3,440 acres. Pop. 1,437. The Barnesley canal terminates at Barnby bridge, in the parish.
CAWTHORPE, a hamlet in the p. of Bourne, Lincolnshire. Pop. 94.
CAWTHORPE (LITTLE), a parish of Lincolnshire, 3 m. SSE of Louth. Area 780 acres. Pop. 196.
CAWTON, a township in the p. of Gilling, N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. from Helmesley. Area 900 acres. Pop. 101.
CAXA-DE-MUERTOS, or THE COFFIN, an islet of the West Indies, half-way between the SE and SW points of the island of Porto-Rico, in N lat. 17° 50', W long. 66° 30'.

CAXAMARCA, a province and town of Peru, in the intendancy of Truxillo. The prov. comprises a superficies nearly 120 m. in length from NW to SE; bounded on the N by New Grenada; on the E by the prov. of Chachapoyas; on the SE by that of Pataz; on the S by the prov. of Guamachuco; and on the W by that of Lambeque. It is intersected on the W by the Andes, and on the E by the Tunguragua and its tributaries. This prov., from the irregularity of its surface, exhibits the greatest variety of climate; but its soil is generally fertile, producing grain in great abundance, cotton, lint, quinquina, and numerous medicinal herbs, and affords excellent pasture to large herds of cattle. It contains mines of gold, silver, copper, lead, and sulphur; and has manufactories of common cloth, linen and cotton fabrics, and sail-cloth. The trade consists chiefly in grain and in articles of local manufacture. Pop., Indian and Spanish, about 46,000. Previous to the Conquest it is said to have contained upwards of 500 Indian tribes; now they do not number above 46. The town of C., the cap. of the prov., is situated on a river of the same name, 80 m. NE of Truxillo, in S lat. 7° 8' 38", W long. 78° 36' 15". It lies in a fertile and salubrious valley of an oval form, probably the bottom of an ancient lake [Humboldt], on the above sea-level. The pop. is estimated at 7,000; and consists chiefly of Indians and Mestizoes. The streets are straight and spacious; the houses are built of clay; but the parish-church, which is of stone, is a large and handsome edifice. It possesses considerable manufactories of woollen, linen, and cotton fabrics, of arms, and of a variety of articles in iron and other metals, and an active commerce. It contains the ruins of the ancient palace of the Incas, and of the chapel where Atahuallpa was basely put to death on the 29th of August, 1553; and about a mile distant are the thermal springs called the Banos-del- Inca or the 'Baths of the Incas,' of a temp. of 156°, and still much frequented. The name of the town was originally Cassamarca or Kazamarca, that is, 'the Frost town.'
CAXAMARQUILLA, a town of Peru, in the dep. of Janin, on the road from Pasco to Huanco, in the prov. of Pataz.

CAXATAMBO, a province and town of Peru, in the intendancy of Junin. The prov. is bounded on the N by the prov. of Huayas, Huari, and Huamalies; on the E by that of Huanco; on the S by the intendancy of Lima; and on the W by the prov. of Santa; comprising an area 120 m. in length, and about equal in breadth. It is intersected by the Andes; and, excepting in the valleys, possesses little warmth of climate. It produces, however, grain and fruit in considerable quantities, and abounds with cattle and sheep. The wool of the latter and cochineal form the chief articles of its commerce.—The cap., of the same name, is situated on the E side of the Andes, 150 m. NNE of Caxatambo.
CAXIAS, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, on the Tagus, and near Lisbon. It contains a royal castle.

CAXIAS, or Caxias-das-Aldeas-Altas, a town of Brazil, cap. of a comarca of the same name, in the prov. of Maranham, on the r. bank of the Itapicuru, 150 m. SSE of Maranham or San Luis, and 125 m. WNW of Oeiras. It was originally an Indian settlement, but was erected into a town by the Portuguese in 1811. It possesses a considerable trade in rice and cotton; and is now one of the most important towns in the prov. The Itapicuru is navigable to the port of Rozario, which lies between the town of that name and C.—The comarca of C., originally called Aldeas-Altas, extends from the Parnahiba on the E to the Mearim on the W, and is conterminous on the N and S with the districts of Codo and Passagem-Franca. Agriculture and the rearing of cattle form the chief employments of its inhabitants.
CAXINES (CAPE). See AKKONADA.

CAXO, an island of Turkey in Asia, 2 m. SW of the island of Scarpanto, in N lat. 35° 18' 20", E long. 26° 52'. It is about 6 m. in length; and though generally barren and rocky, produces a little corn and wine. There is a v. of about 100 houses on a little bay on its N side.
CAXOEIRA. See CACHOEIRA.
CAXO-PULO, a small island of Turkey in Asia, in the archipelago, to the N of the island of Caxo, in N lat. 35° 28′, E long. 26° 55′.
CAXORROS, an island of Brazil, in the bay of Angra-dos-Reis, prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro.
CAXTON, a parish of Cambridgeshire, 50 m. NNW of London. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. 558.

CAXTON, a township of Lower Canada, in the co. of St. Maurice, watered by several rivers and lakes, among which are Lac-Cananshing and Lac des-Perchaudes. It is in some parts hilly, and is generally fertile. The wood consists of maple, birch, beech, ash, cedar, spruce, &c.

CAYA, a river of Portugal, in the prov. of Alentejo, which takes its rise in the Serra-Alpedreira, in CAY the comarca of Portalegre; crosses that of Alvas; and after a total course of 42 m., falls into the Guadiana on the confines of Spain, and 4 m. WSW of Badajoz.
CAYAA, a lake of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, division of Rio-Negro, near the l. bank and connected with the Madeira.
CAYACANCA, a cataract of the Curitiba, in Brazil, in the prov. of Santa-Catharina.

CAYACARA, a town of Venezuela, in the dep. and on the r. bank of the Orinoco, opposite the junction of the Manapire, and 20 m. ENE of that of the Apure.—Also a town 15 m. WSW of the preceding, opposite the confluence of the Mancapra.
CAYAMA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, division of Rio-Negro, which flows into the Amazon, on the r. bank, 40 m. ESE of Ega or Teffe.

CAYAMBE, or Cajambi'-Urcu, a summit of the Andes, in the republic of Ecuador, 45 m. ENE of Quito, nearly under the equinoctial line. It rises in the form of a truncated cone, to the height of 19,548 ft. above sea-level, and is one of the loftiest and most remarkable of the mountain-summits of Quito.
CAYAPOS. See CAIAPO.
CAYARI. See MADEIRA river.

CAYCOS, or Caicos, a numerous group of cays or islands in the Caribbean sea, lying between N lat. 21° and 22°, and W long. 71° 20' to 72° 30'. The principal are East C., or Grand C., of which the NE point is in N lat. 21° 43′, W long. 71° 24′; North C.; Providenciales, or North-west C.; and the Little or West C.; of which the S point is in N lat. 21° 37½, W long. 72° 28½. The entire group had a pop. of 557 in 1847, chiefly engaged in rearing stock and produce for the Turks Islands market.

CAYENNE, an island of French Guayana, separated from the continent of S America by the river Cayenne on the W, and the Mahury or Ouya on the E; and by the canal called Rivière-du-Tour-de-l'Isle, which unites these two rivers. It is low in surface, and does not exceed 36 m. in circumf. The situation of the island makes it a most unfit place for a settlement, and for the capital of the colony. It would probably have fared better with the early colonists if they had fixed their residence on the mainland, which opposite the island is hilly and mountainous, and in this respect is distinguished from the greater part of the Guayana coast, which is for the most part low and swampy, and subject to continual inundations both from the sea and from the rivers which rush impetuously down the mountains. All the productions of the mainland are raised on the island, but with this difference, that the soil of the latter is exhausted, and does not equally repay the planter for his expenses and labour. The number of inhabitants on the island in 1837 was 7,933, of whom 5,023 were slaves. The town is situated in N lat. 4° 56′ 15″, W long. 52° 16½′ [Condamine], close to the sea-side, on the r. bank, and near the mouth of the Cayenne river, which is about a league broad, and generally from 25 to 30 ft. deep. and the W branch of which forms the port, which has not above 3 ft. of water. The older part of it consists of a few wooden and badly - constructed houses, surrounded by a swampy moat, and by wretched walls which form a sort of irregular hexagon. The fort is of earth, and tolerably strong towards the sea, especially as ships cannot approach within cannon-shot of it for want of sufficient depth of water. Ships drawing more than 15 ft. water anchor about 6 m. from C. The navigation of the coast is generally dangerous on account of shoals and flats; and there is not a single good harbour on the whole coast except on the island itself. A new town has lately been built on the neighbouring sa- ヘルトリタス・ vannah, and is separated from the old one merely by the ditch. This—which is the more considerable of the two-is regularly built; has wide streets admitting the free access and circulation of the air; and contains some elegant houses, the beautiful appearance of which becomes more striking from the obvious marks of poverty and misery universally exhibited around. The pop. of the town in 1836 was 5,220, of whom 2,373 were slaves. The tonnage of the port in 1836 amounted to only 20 vessels = 596 tons, all employed in the coasting-trade. See article GUAYANA (French).

CAYES (Les), a town and port of the West Indies, cap. of the dep. du Sud, in the island of Hayti or St. Domingo, on the S coast, opposite the island of Vache, 15 m. WSW of St. Louis and 100 m. WSW of Port-au-Prince, in N lat. 18° 11' 10", W long. 73° 45'. Pop. 3,000; previous to the revolution it amounted to 12,000. It lies in a marshy and unhealthy but fertile district; and possesses a considerable trade in sugar, cotton, coffee, and indigo. This town sustained extensive damage by a hurricane in 1831.
CAYES-JACMEL (Les), a small town and port of the West Indies, in the island of St. Domingo, dep. de l'Ouest, 14 m. E of Jacmel, and 24 m. S of Port-au-Prince, at the mouth of a small river.
CAYEMITES. See CAYMITES.
CAYETANO-ISLANDS, a group off the E coast of Patagonia, near Cape Sur, in S lat. 53° 53', W long. 72° 09'.

CAYEUX, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Somme, cant. of Saint-Valery-sur- Somme, on the l. bank and near the entrance of the Somme into the straits of Dover, and 19 m. W of Abbeville. Pop. in 1841, 2,796. It has a small fishing-port.

CAYLAR (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Herault, arrond. of Lodive .- The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 3,644; in 1841, 3,452. The town is situated on a rocky plateau, 11 m. N of Lodive. Pop. 824.

CAYLUS (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Tarn - et - Garonne, arrond. of Montauban. The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,594; in 1841, 9,928. The town, which is of considerable antiquity, is on the r. bank of the Bonnette, 28 m. NE of Montauban. Pop. in 1841, 5,152; in 1846, 5,402. It possesses a considerable trade in grain, and has 11 annual fairs.
CAYMAN, a small river of S Africa, in the district of George, flowing into the sea 10 m. SE of George.
CAYMAN (LAKE), a lake of Mexico, in the prov. and 150 m. WSW of Cohahuila, in the desert of Bolson - de - Mapimi. It discharges its waters by the river Nasos.

CAYMANS, three islands in the Caribbean sea, about 30 leagues NNW of Point Negril, between 79° 45' and 81° 24' W. long., forming a dependency of Jamaica. The NE point of Grand C. is in N lat. 19° 22', W long. 81° 7'. It is about 1\frac{1}{2} m. in length and 1 m. broad, and is the only one inhabited. It is low in surface, the average height above sea-level being about 25 ft.; and is covered with cocoa-nut trees. It has an anchorage on the SW side. Its soil is fertile, producing grain and arrow-root. The pop. in 1827 was estimated at 1,600; in 1834, at 2,016; who are chiefly employed in catching turtle for the supply of Jamaica. On the W side of this island is the v. of Georgetown; on the S, Boddentown.—The other two islands, C. Branque and Little C., are within 5 m. of each other, and about 34 m. N of Grand C. CAYMANS, CAIMANS, or VIVORILLAS, a cluster | NNE of Grantham, intersected by the Brant. Area - CAY of rocks in the Caribbean sea, near the Mosquito coast, in N lat. 15° 50', W long. 83° 8'.
CAY MARINO, two islets in the China sea, one of which lies in about N lat. 5° 50', 75 m. NW of Labuan; and the other in about N lat. 8° 30', 150 m. WNW of Balabac.
CAYMEN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of East Prussia, and 15 m. NE of Königsberg.

CAYMITES (THE), two islands off the coast of Hayti, distinguished as Grand and Petite C. - Grand C. has its E point in N lat. 18° 37', W long. 73° 39'; and is 5 m. in length from E to W, with a breadth of 3½ m. Its highest point is 500 ft. above sea-level. The Petite C. lies to the NW of the Grand C.
CAYO, a small but conspicuous isle in the Bissao channel, 13 leagues SSE of Cape Roxo, on the Senegambian coast.

CAYON, a town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. and 9 m. W of Corunna, and partido of San-Juan-de- Carballo, at the entrance of a small river into the Atlantic.—Also a small river in the island of St. Christopher, in the p. of St. Mary, which falls into the sea on the E coast, to the SE of Madan-point.
CAYOR. See KAYOR.
CAYO SAL, or SALT KAY, an islet on the SW side of the Anguilla bank, in N lat. 23° 39' 8", W long. 80° 17′ 38″.

CAYO VERDE, or GREEN KAY, an islet at the SE extremity of the Great Bahama bank, in N lat. 22° 1′, W long. 75° 10′. It rises to a height of 72 ft. above the sea. Its length does not exceed 3 m.; and its only vegetation consists of wild fig-trees, prickly pear, and a kind of camphire.
CAYPHA. See KAFFA.

CAYRES, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Loire, arrond. of Le Puy. The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 4,584; in 1841, 4,534. The village is 10 m. SSW of Le Puy. Pop. 1,179.
CAYRIRIS, or Cairiris (Serra). See Jabitaca.
CAYROS (POINT), a headland of Spain, in Galicia, prov. of Lugo, 10 m. WNW of Ribadeo, in W long. 7° 10'.

CAYRU, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, comarca of Valença, in S lat. 13° 27', W long. 39° 5'. It is situated on a small island of the same name, formed by the rivers Tinhare and Tupiaçu, about 15 m. above their entrance into the Atlantic. The principal streets are paved, and contain many largesized houses, two churches, an initiatory and classical school, and a convent.—The district of C. comprises, in addition to the island of the same name, the adjacent islands of Morro and Galliao, and abounds with excellent timber. Pop. 800.

CAYSTER, or KUCHUK - MENDER [i. e., 'Little Mender], a river of Asiatic Turkey, which rises in the Bos-dagh, or Tmolus, and flows in a winding but prevailingly W course through the sanjak of Aidin, to the gulf of Skalanova, into which it flows at a point a little to the N of Ephesus. It drains the country enclosed between the Bos-dagh and its prolongations to the head of the gulf of Smyrna, on the N, and the range of Messogis on the S. The latter range separates it from the basin of the Maander or Bojuk-Mender. It is the Caystrus or Caystros of the ancients.

CAYTE, or CAITE, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, forming the outlet of several lakes which lie about 20 m. from the coast. It passes Bragança, formerly called Caite, and falls into a bay of the same name. It is navigable at high-water to the town of B.
CAYTHORPE, a parish of Lincolnshire, 9 m. 3,820 acres. Pop. in 1841, 821.—Also a township in the p. of Lowdham, Nottinghamshire, 8 m. NNE of Nottingham, near the Trent. Pop. 315.
CAYTON, a parish and township in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SE of Scarborough, on the coast. Area 2,430 acres. Pop. in 1841, 572. Area of township 1,140 acres. Pop. 69.

CAYUBABAS, a lake of Bolivia, in the group of the lakes or lagunes of the Mamore, which discharges itself into that river at San Jose. It is supposed to be 36 m. in length, and 21 m. in breadth. The surrounding district is inhabited by a tribe of unsubdued Indians of the same name.
CAYUELA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and partido of Burgos.

CAYUGA, a township of Upper Canada, in the Niagara district. Pop. in 1841, 837. It is intersected by the Grand river, the banks of which abound with timber, chiefly white oak. It contains a village of the same name, pleasantly situated on the Grand river, 15 m. above Dunnville, and on the road from Simcoe to the Falls of the Niagara. Pop. about 70. In the vicinity is a bed of white gypsum. Timber forms the chief article of local trade.—Also a county in the state of New York, U.S., comprising a surface of 648 sq. m., bordered on the N by Lake Ontario, and intersected by Seneca river, the Erie canal, and Western railroad. In the centre is Owasco lake; on the E border, Skeneateles lake; and on the W a lake of the same name. It contains also several salt and sulphur springs; the former are very extensive, and gypsum. The soil is generally productive. Pop. in 1830, 47,948; in 1840, 50,338. Cap., Auburn.—Also a village in the above co., in Aurelius township, 162 m. NW of Albany, on the NE part of the lake of the same name, which is here crossed by a bridge 1,850 paces in length, and a railroad bridge still longer. Pop. in 1840, 300. The lake, which lies between Seneca and Tompkins counties, is 40 m. in length, and from 1 to 3 m. broad. It is very deep, and discharges itself into Seneca river. It contains fine fish, and its shores are highly cultivated. A steam-boat communicates between C. bridge and Ithaca, at the opposite extremity of the lake.

CAYUTA, a township of Chemung co., in the state of New York, U. S., 188 m. WSW of Albany. It presents a hilly surface, and is drained by a creek of the same name and its tributaries. Pine and hemlock form its principal growth. Pop. in 1840, 835.
CAYZA, a town of Bolivia, in the prov. and 40 m. SE of Potosi, near the l. bank of the Gura.—Also a town of La Plata, in the Llanos de Manso, on the r. bank of the Rio-Pilcomayo or Araguai Guazu.
CAZAL. See CASAL.

CAZALA-DE-LA-SIERRA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 36 m. NNE of Seville. It is situated in the mountains of Constantina, between the Guezna and Biar, and is of great antiquity. Pop. 13,072. It contains several churches, convents, and hospitals, and has manufactories of cloth. A fair is held here annually, the trade of which consists chiefly in cattle, and in articles of local manufacture. The surrounding district affords good wine; and in the adjacent mountains are mines of silver, and in small quantities, of copper and iron. the prov. and 13 m. NNW of Jaen, and partido of Andujar, between the rivers Jaen and Guadalquiver.
CAZALIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, cant. of Hagetmau. Pop. 307. The environs afford good white wine.

CAZALS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Lot, arrond. of Cahors. The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,390; in 1841, pueblos. CAZ 7,536. The town is in a valley 18 m. NW of Cahors. Pop. 793.

CAZAL-VASCO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, on the r. bank of the Guapore or Itenez, near the confluence of the Allegro, and 20 m. S of Villa-Bella. Pop., exclusive of the garrison, 400.
CAZAN. See KAZAN.
CAZANARE. See CASANARE.
CAZAR. See KASR.
CAZAU, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. and com. and 8 m. S of La-Teste-de- Buch, near the great lagune of the same name. Pop. 130.

CAZAUBON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Gers. The cant. comprises 18 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,583; in 1841, 13,096. The town is 27 m. W of Condom, on the r. bank of the Douze. Pop. 2,657. It contains extensive distilleries of brandy distinguished by the name of 'Armagnac.'
CAZAUX-LAYRISSE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. of Saint-Beat. Pop. 222. It contains some copper-mines.
CAZE (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of the Tarn, cant. and 10 m. WNW of La Caune. Pop. 2,074.
CAZEGUT, or CAJEGU'T, an island of Senegambia, in the group of the Bijuga or Bissagos islands, in N lat. 11° 30', W long. 16°.
CAZEMBE. See KAZEMBA.

CAZENOVIA, a township of Madison co., in the state of New York, U. S., 113 m. NW of Albany. It is generally hilly, and is watered by Chittenango creek, which flows N into Oneida lake. In the centre is a lake of the same name. The soil consists of a fertile gravelly loam. Pop. in 1840, 4,153.

CAZE'RES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, arrond. of Muret. The cant. comprises 18 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,753; in 1841, 11,938. The town is situated on the 1. bank of the Garonne, 27 m. SW of Muret. Pop. in 1841, 2,471. It contains manufactories of woollen fabrics, hosiery, and candles, tanneries and dyeworks, and has three annual fairs. A fine promenade separates the suburbs from the town.—Also a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Landes, cant. of Grenade-sur-l'Adour, on the r. bank of the Adour, 11 m. SE of Mont-de-Marsan. Pop. 962. It contains manufactories of woollen fabrics and hats, tanneries and dye-works. Cloth forms its chief article of commerce.
CAZERES. See CACERES.
CAZES-MONDENARD, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Tarn-et-Garonne, cant. of Lauzerte. Pop. in 1841, 3,002.
CAZIGA, a town of Sudan, in the kingdom of Houssa, 80 m. E of Guari.
CAZILLAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Martel. Pop. 1,034.
CAZIQUARE. See Cassiquiari.
CAZIQUEI, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Saő- Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, which falls into the Ibicui, near the confluence of the Toropi.
CAZLA, a town of Turkey in Asia, in Anadolia, in the sanjak of Sarukhan, on the gulf of Sanderli, 35 m. N of Smyrna.
CAZONES, a bay of the S coast of the island of
CAZALILLA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in | Cuba, in N lat. 22° 10', W long. 81° 35'.

CAZORLA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 42 m. E of Jaen, on the Vega, in a valley of the sierra of the same name. Pop. 7,383. It contains a church and several convents. The rearing of cattle and working of the adjacent salt springs, form the chief employments of the inhabitants. The partido comprises 13 #
CAZOULS-LES-BE'ZIERS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. and 7 m. NW of Beziers. Pop. in 1841, 2,110. A good muscat wine is grown in the locality.
CAZZIOLA, or CAZZIOLI, a small island of Dalmatia, 4 m. W of the island of Lagosta.
CAZZO, or CAZZA, an islet of Dalmatia, 6 m. WNW of the islet of Cazziola, in N lat. 42° 46', E long. 16° 3'.

CEA, a river of Spain, which takes its rise in the NE of the prov. of Leon; runs S, passing Morgobejo, Almanza, Cea, and Sahagún; thence bends SW through the prov. of Valladolid, passing Mayorga, and re-entering the prov. of Leon, falls, after a total course of 90 m., into the Esla, 10 m. WSW of Balderas, and 3 m. ENE of Benevente.-Also a town in the prov. and 30 m. ESE of Leon, partido and 7 m. N of Sahagun, on the l. bank of the river of the same name. Pop. 1,200.—Also a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 32 m. WSW of Guarda, on the Serra d'Estrella. Pop. 1,400. It contains a church, an hospital, and an alms-house. See also KEA.

CEA (SAN-CRISTO'BAL-DE), a town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. and 10 m. NW of Orense, partido of Senorin-de-Carballino. Pop. 1,000. CEARA', CIARA, or SEARA, a maritime province of Brazil; bounded on the N by the Atlantic; on the E by the provinces of Rio-Grande-No-Norte and Parahiba; on the S by the prov. of Pernambuco, from which it is separated by the Sierra Borborêma; and on the W by the prov. of Piauhi, from which it is separated by the Sierra Hibiapaba and its northern prolongation. Its area is estimated in the Dicc. Geog. do imp. do Brazil at about 4,600 Portuguese sq. leagues. Schæffer estimated it at 3,311 German sq. m.; but this admeasurement evidently comprises territories now annexed to other provinces. In the Weimar Almanach for 1848, the area is stated at 1,600 German sq. m. or 33,980 English sq. m. The surface rises gradually from the coast towards the S; and all its important rivers have a course from S to N. The principal rivers are the Jaguaribe, the Ceará, the Mandahu, and the Camozim. Cotton, fruits, gums, dye-woods, salt, cattle, and hides, are the principal articles of export. Its cattle are highly esteemed. The carnauba palm (Coriphera cerifera) is abundant in this prov., and furnishes the Indian pop. at once with food, houses and raiment; and the melancia or water-melon is produced here in unwonted profusion and great excellence.—The pop. was estimated in 1831 at 160,000; in 1843 at 180,000. A large proportion consists of Indians in a state of semi-barbarism. In 1841, the number of primary schools was 31 with 830 pupils; and 7 Latin schools with 46 pupils.—The prov. is divided into 8 comarcas, and sends 8 deputies to the general legislative assembly. Its provincial assembly is composed of 4 senators, and 28 deputies.—Its principal towns are the cap., of the same name, Aquiraz, Aracati, Botarite, Cascavel, Crato, Granja, Icó, Imperatiz, Inhamuz, Jardim, Lavras, Quixeramobim, Rio-de-Sangue, San- Bernardo, San-Matheos, Sobral, Januaria, Vicosa, and Villanova d'el Rei.

CEARA', sometimes denominated FORTALEZA, the cap. of the above prov., is in S lat. 3° 42′ 58″, W long. 38° 34'. It stands 7 m. NW of the mouth of the river of the same name, upon a bay opening to the N. It is well planned, with wide streets, and ample squares; but has no public buildings of any importance; and what is more remarkable, does not contain a convent, nor any edifice devoted to monastic purposes. "C.," says Kidder, "is literally a city built upon the sand. From the beach to the remotest suburb all is sand, sand." Sand fills the streets, | slaves, and 2,551 free coloured. - Also a township of CEC and composes the side-walks; walking, riding, or driving, all are equally impeded in C. by the sand; and for the locomotion of a single cart, it is not unusual to employ five pair of oxen. The harbour is protected and screened by a reef of rocks under water; but is said to be annually filling-up with sand. A lighthouse on Mocoripe point, in N lat. 3° 41', W long. 38° 30′, was erected in 1847.

CEARA', a river of Brazil, in the prov. of the same name, which rises in the mountains to the S of the city of C., and flows into the ocean at Villa- Velha, near Mocoripe point. It is a small stream, scarcely navigable by canoes; and has a sand-bar across its mouth.

CEARA'-MIRIM, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-Grande-do-Norte, which descends from the Serra Borborêma, runs NE, and falls into the Atlantic to the N of the embouchure of the Grande, in N lat. 37° 34′ 53". It is navigable for some distance for canoes.
CEARCY, a township of Phillip's co., in the state of Arkansas, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 359.
CEAUCE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Dromfront. Pop. in 1841, 3,428.
CEAULEMONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Indre, cant. of Eguzon. Pop. 1,107.
CEAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Ducey, on the English channel. Pop. 815. It contains numerous salt-works.
CEAUX-D'ALLE'GRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Loire, cant. of Allegre, 12 m. NW of Le Puy. Pop. 1,495.
CEBAZAT, or CEBASSAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. and 3 m. N of Clermont-Ferrand. Pop. in 1841, 2,126.
CEBOLAS, a parish of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, district of Parahiba-do-Sul, in the Serra-dos-Orgaos.

CEBOLLA, or CEVOLLA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 30 m. W of Toledo, on the side of a hill, near the r. bank of the Tagus. Pop. 2,800. It contains two churches, a palace belonging to the dukes of Alba, and an hospital. It was formerly a town of considerable importance. The environs produce good wine.

CEBOLLERA (SIERRA), a mountain-range of Spain, in Old Castile, which extends about 12 m. SE on the confines of the prov. of Soria and Burgos. Its highest peak has an alt. of 6,929 ft. above sea-level.
CEBOLLEROS, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 42 m. NNE of Burgos, partido and 9 m. ESE of Villarcayo, near the l. bank of the Nela.
CEBREROS, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 30 m. SE of Avila, near the N bank of the Alberche, which is here crossed by a stone bridge. Pop. 2,744.

CEBRET, a mountain of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. of Pontevedra, comarca of Lemos, 13 m. E of Vigo, which contains a fountain named Louzana, near the source of the Loriz, which is said to ebb and flow, and to become warm and cold alternately.
CEBRONES-DEL-RIO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 28 m. SSW of Leon, partido and 5 m. SE of La Baneza, on the l. bank of the Orvigo.
CECCANO, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation and 5 m. S of Frosinone, near the r. bank of the Sacco. Pop. 3,800.

CECIL, a county in the state of Maryland, U. S., comprising an area of 264 sq. m., bordered on the W by the Susquehanna and Chesapeake bay, and intersected by the Elk river and the Chesapeake and Delaware canal. It presents an undulating surface, and is tolerably fertile. Pop. in 1830, 15,432; in 1840, 17,232, of whom 13,329 were whites, 1,352 * Washington co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 10 m. NE of Washington. It is intersected by Millar's branch of Chartier's creek, and exhibits considerable variety of surface. Its soil is loamy, and it abounds with coal. Pop. in 1840, 1,027. Cap., Elkton.
CECILE (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. of Bollène. Pop. in 1841, 2,080.—Also a commune in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Les Essarts. Pop. 1,620.
CECILIA-DEL-ALCOR (SANTA), a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and partido and 9 m. SW of Palencia, on the r. bank of the Salon.
CECILSTOWN, a village in the p. of Castle- Magner, co. Cork, 6 m. WNW of Mallow. Pop. 346.

CECINA, a river of Tuscany, which takes its rise in the prov. of Sienna, to the N of Montieri, at an alt. of 1,903 ft. above sea-level; runs NNW; thence bends W through the prov. of Pisa; intersects the maremma of Pisa and Volterra; and after a total course of 42 m., flows into the Mediterranean near the village of the same name, and 22 m. SSE of Leghorn. Its principal affluent is the Stezza.

CECINA, or FITTO-DE-CECINA, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the prov. and maremma of Pisa, near the mouth of the river of the same name, and 20 m. WSW of Volterra. Pop. 300. This village, which is of recent origin, contains a copper-foundry. In the environs is a grand ducal villa formerly belonging to the family of Medici.

CECLAVIN, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. and 32 m. NW of Caceres, partido and 10 m. NE of Alcantara, in a hilly district near the Ribera, which is here crossed by a bridge. Pop. 3,090. It is of considerable antiquity, and was formerly extremely flourishing.

CEDAR, a county in the state of Iowa, U. S., watered by a creek of the same name and Wabesepinica river. Pop. in 1840, 1,253. Cap., Tipton.— Also a township of Boone co., in the state of Missouri. Pop. 3,313.—Also a township of Callaway co., in the same state. Pop. 1,896.
CEDAR BERGEN, a mountain-range of South Africa, in the Cape territory, district of Clanwilliam. It extends S between the Elephant and Doorn rivers.

CEDAR CAYS, three small islets, on the W side of Vacasausa bay, in the gulf of Mexico, on the W coast of Florida, in N lat. 29° 20', W long. 83° 16'. The formation of a road across the N part of the peninsular part of Florida would shorten the sea-route between New York and New Orleans nearly 1,000 m., besides rendering it unnecessary for mercantile craft to encounter the dangers of the Florida reefs. The terminus of the proposed road on the E or Atlantic side of Florida might be St. Mary's; and the terminus on the gulf might be opposite the C. cays. The distance between the two points would be about 140 m.; and the proposed road would bring the two cities of New York and Orleans within four days' journey of each other.

CEDAR CREEK, a township of Cooper co., in the state of Missouri, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 664.—Also a village of Sussex co., in the state of Delaware. Pop. 2,420.—Also a township of Allan co., in the state of Indiana. Pop. 189.—Also a river of Rockbridge co., in the state of Virginia, which, after a course of 9 m., falls into James river, 13 m. SSW of Lexington. It is crossed near the confluence by a natural bridge of massive rock, crowned with trees.

CEDAR LAKE, a lake of British North America, in Cumberland. It forms an expansion of the Sascatchawan from 6 to 12 m. in breadth, and lies about 20 m. W of the entrance of that river into Lake Winnipeg. Its waters abound with fish, and its banks with game. CEF
CEDARTOWN, a village, cap. of Paulding co., in the state of Georgia, U.S., on the N side of Tallapoosa river, 151 m. NW of Milledgeville.

CEDARVILLE, a village of Litchfield township, Herkimer co., in the state of New York, U. S., 79 m. NW of Albany.—Also a village of Fairfield township, Cumberland co., in the state of New Jersey, on Cedar creek, 4 m. from its mouth, and 75 m. SW of Trenton. It contains about 60 dwellings.
CEDEGOLO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the Val Camonica, on the l. bank of the Oglio, 9 m. N of Breno.
CEDEIRA, or CEDEYRA, a town and port of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. and 24 m. NNE of Corunna, and partido of Santa-Marta-de-Ortigueira, on a bay of the same name.
CEDILLO, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 18 m. N of Toledo, partido and 5 m. WSW of Illescas.
CEDILLO-DE-LA-TORRE, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 38 m. NE of Segovia, and partido of Riaza, on the l. bank of the Caravias.
CEDRES (DES). See SOULANGE.
CEDRILLAS, a town of Aragon, in the prov. and partido and W of Teruel.
CEDRIS, a township, partly in the parishes of Llanfihangel-y-Penant and Talyllyn, Merionethshire. Pop. in 1841, 166.
CEE, a town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. and 56 m. WSW of Corunna, partido of San-Marcos-de- Corcubion, on the bay of Corcubion.
CEFALONIA. See CEPHALONIA.

CEFALU, a town of Sicily, cap. of a district of the same name, in the prov. and 42 m. ESE of Palerme, on the Mediterranean. Pop. 9,000. It is situated on a rocky cape, and is enclosed by a wall. It contains a castle, a fine cathedral, several churches, and a custom-house, and has a small port. The district comprises 6 cant.
CEFEN-COCH, a township in the p. of Llanrhaiadr-yn-Mochnant, Cardiganshire. Pop. in 1841, 331.
CEFENPAWL, a township in the p. of Abbeycwm-hir, Radnorshire. Pop. in 1841, 139.
CEFN, a hamlet in the p. of Gelli-gaer, Glamorganshire. Pop. in 1841, 611.
CEFN-CYMER, a township in the p. of Llangwym, Denbighshire. Pop. in 1841, 119.
CEFN-LLYS-ISAF, a township in the p. of Llanerfyl, Montgomeryshire. Pop. in 1841, 192.
CEFN - LLYS - UCHAF, a township in the p. of Llanerfyl, Montgomeryshire. Pop. in 1841, 223.
CEFN-Y-COED, a township in the p. of Eglwysfach, Denbighshire. Pop. in 1841, 290.
CEFNBLAIDD, a hamlet in the p. of Talley, Carmarthenshire. Pop. in 1841, 376.
CEFNDU, a township in the p. of Rhuddlan, Flintshire. Pop. in 1841, 113.
CEFNHAFODAN, a township in the p. of Llangirrig, Montgomeryshire. Pop. in 1841, 336.
CEFNLLYNOG, a township in the p. of Meifod or Myfod, Montgomeryshire. Pop. in 1841, 52.
